planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sunny position.

directions: This Spanish variety produces deeply lobed leaves and small very dark figs with intense sweet-red flesh. Give it the warmest, sunniest site and lean, well-drained soil for the best flavor concentration. Harvest when the near-black fruit softens and droops fully.

planting.note: Set out after frost in full sun with good drainage.

directions: A compact, container-friendly variety bearing dark figs with sweet amber-red flesh. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist through the fruiting flush. Harvest when the figs soften and the necks bend under their own weight.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny location.

directions: This California-grown variety yields large figs with greenish-brown skin and strawberry-red pulp. Give it full sun, deep well-drained soil, and consistent summer water for plump fruit. Harvest when the figs soften and hang down, catching the main crop in late summer.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into full sun.

directions: Also known as Green Greek, this heirloom carries green-skinned figs with sweet light-amber flesh. Plant in full sun and free-draining soil, watering steadily as fruit swells. Harvest when the skin softens and shows a slight yellow tinge, since the fruit stays green when ripe.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: This Catalan variety is noted for producing up to three crops in a long, warm season. Site it in full sun with sharp drainage and feed lightly in spring. Harvest the sweet dark figs across the season as each flush softens and droops.

planting.note: Plant in spring; suited to cooler climates and container culture.

directions: This French variety bears two crops, an early breba and a main autumn crop, and adapts well to pots and cooler summers. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil and protect from hard winter cold. Harvest the pale greenish-brown figs with sweet amber flesh as they soften.

planting.note: Set out in spring in full sun after frost.

directions: Also sold as Melette, this variety ripens small yellow-green figs early in the season. Plant in full sun with free-draining soil and keep steadily watered. Harvest when the fruit softens and the flesh turns sweet pink-amber inside.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a traditional northern French variety for cooler areas.

directions: Long grown around Paris, this variety ripens green-yellow figs even in short, cool summers. Give it the warmest wall you can, full sun, and well-drained soil. Harvest when the fruit softens and shows a honeyed amber interior.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a warm, sunny spot.

directions: This French variety is named for the golden drop of nectar that beads at the eye of ripe fruit. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and water evenly during fruit development. Harvest the yellow-green figs when soft and weeping a sugary drop.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: This French variety produces large violet-brown breba figs early, followed by a main crop. Give it full sun, fertile well-drained soil, and steady moisture for the big early fruit. Harvest when the figs soften and the necks droop.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny, sheltered location.

directions: A late-ripening Provencal variety with dark figs and deep red, richly flavored flesh. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, allowing plenty of heat to finish the crop. Harvest in early autumn when the near-black figs soften completely.

planting.note: Set out after frost in full sun; a compact selection good for pots.

directions: This compact variety bears elongated dark figs with jammy, berry-like red flesh and works well in containers. Plant in full sun with free-draining soil and keep evenly watered. Harvest when the fruit softens fully and the neck bends over.

planting.note: Plant in spring; reliable and productive in cool-summer climates.

directions: An English variety valued for heavy, dependable crops of pale greenish-white figs even in marginal climates. Grow in full sun against a warm wall in well-drained soil. Harvest when the fruit softens and the amber flesh sweetens.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Southern heirloom tolerant of humidity.

directions: This old Georgia variety carries small dull-bronze figs with a closed eye that resists spoilage in wet, humid summers. Plant in full sun with good drainage and mulch the roots. Harvest the sweet amber-fleshed figs as they soften on the branch.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with good drainage.

directions: This variety ripens sweet brown figs with rich, honeyed amber flesh over a long season. Plant in full sun and free-draining soil, watering steadily as fruit swells. Harvest when the figs soften and the necks droop.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a hot, sunny site.

directions: A small-fruited Mediterranean variety bearing very dark figs with sweet, deep-flavored flesh. Give it the hottest sunny spot and lean, well-drained soil to build sugars. Harvest when the near-black fruit softens and hangs down.

planting.note: Set out only in frost-free tropical or subtropical conditions once well warmed.

directions: Sow the tiny seeds thinly on the surface of moist, warm seed mix and keep humid until the fine seedlings appear. This large tropical tree produces edible reddish figs directly on its trunk and main branches, so allow generous space near water. Harvest the clustered fruit when it turns red and softens.

planting.note: Plant out only where frost is absent and warmth is reliable.

directions: Surface-sow seed on warm, moist mix and keep humid until germination, or root large cuttings. Known for enormous rounded leaves, this Himalayan tree bears clusters of flattened reddish figs low on the trunk. Harvest the fruit when it flushes red and softens for cooking or fresh eating.

planting.note: Grow only in frost-free warm regions with ample room.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of warm, moist mix and keep bright and humid until seedlings establish. This broad, spreading African and Middle Eastern tree bears clusters of small edible figs along its branches. Harvest when the fruit yellows or reddens and softens.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this Himalayan wild fig tolerates more cold than tropical species.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on warm, moist mix, or start from cuttings for named fruit quality. A close wild relative of the common fig, it produces small sweet purple figs and hand-shaped leaves. Harvest when the fruit softens and darkens on the branch.

planting.note: Plant out in frost-free, sheltered conditions near moisture.

directions: Sow seed on warm, moist mix and keep humid, or strike softwood cuttings. This Australian small tree has rough sandpapery leaves and bears edible purple figs on short stalks. Harvest when the fruit turns dark purple and softens, eating fresh or in preserves.

planting.note: Set out in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant this vigorous Mediterranean orange in full sun with the graft union well above grade. It appreciates deep, infrequent irrigation and a spring feeding of citrus fertilizer. The thick-rinded, nearly seedless fruit peels easily and ripens through winter; clip the stems when the oranges are fully colored and aromatic.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after the ground warms.

directions: This seedless Spanish juice orange grows best in full sun with well-drained soil and protection from hard frost. Keep the soil evenly moist and fertilize through spring and summer for good fruit set. The exceptionally juicy oranges ripen in midwinter and hold their quality on the tree, so harvest as the flavor peaks.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost.

directions: An early-bearing sport of the Washington navel, this compact tree fruits young and heavily on a smaller frame. Plant in full sun with the graft high, water deeply, and feed with citrus fertilizer in spring. The seedless navels ripen a bit earlier than standard navels; clip them when fully colored and sweet.

planting.note: Transplant in spring in warm soil.

directions: This Valencia sport bears juice with deeper orange color and lower acidity than the standard. Grow in full sun with excellent drainage and keep steady moisture through the warm season. The fruit ripens late spring to summer and holds well on the tree, so harvest once the flavor is fully developed.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: This mandarin-orange hybrid combines rich, spicy flavor with an easy-peeling rind. Plant in full sun with good drainage and shelter from cold, as it is more frost-sensitive than many mandarins. The fruit ripens in winter; harvest when deeply colored and fragrant, clipping to avoid tearing the rind.

planting.note: Transplant in spring in a frost-free site.

directions: A Jamaican tangor with the sweetness of a mandarin and the juiciness of an orange, Ortanique grows into a vigorous, productive tree. Give it full sun, deep watering, and regular feeding through the growing season. The fruit ripens late winter into spring and holds well on the tree; harvest once fully colored and sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered location.

directions: This large, distinctively top-knotted mandarin hybrid is exceptionally sweet and seedless. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and protect from frost, as the fruit needs a long, warm ripening period. The oversized fruit matures late winter into spring; harvest when the rind is fully orange and pulls loose easily.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: This richly flavored, deep-orange mandarin is nearly seedless when grown away from other pollen sources. Give it full sun, well-drained soil, and steady moisture with regular feeding. The easy-peeling fruit ripens late winter into spring; harvest once the color is deep and the flavor has fully sweetened.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny spot.

directions: One of the latest-ripening mandarins, Encore holds its sweet, richly flavored fruit into late spring and summer. Grow in full sun with excellent drainage and give consistent water through the warm season. The fruit contains seeds but peels readily; harvest over an extended window once fully colored and sweet.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost.

directions: This bright orange, richly flavored mandarin sets heavy crops and colors early. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and thin overloaded branches to improve fruit size. Fruit ripens in early to mid winter and contains seeds; harvest when deeply colored and the flesh tastes sweet and aromatic.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: An early-ripening tangelo-type mandarin with sweet, deep-orange flesh and a thin, easy-peeling rind. Give it full sun and good drainage, and plant a compatible pollinator nearby for reliable set. Fruit ripens in early winter and can be seedy; harvest when fully colored and richly flavored.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm site.

directions: This sweet, smooth-skinned mandarin hybrid produces best with a nearby pollinator to boost fruit set. Plant in full sun with free-draining soil and keep it steadily watered and fed. The juicy, pale-orange fruit ripens in early winter; harvest when fully colored and the flavor is sweet.

planting.note: Transplant in spring in warm soil.

directions: A late-season mandarin with rich, tangy-sweet flavor and deep orange, easy-peeling fruit. Plant in full sun with good drainage and give consistent moisture through the warm months. Fruit ripens late winter into spring and can be seedy; harvest once fully colored and flavorful.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger.

directions: A cross of Owari satsuma and King mandarin, Kara bears large, aromatic, sprightly-sweet fruit. Give it full sun and well-drained soil, and provide steady water for good fruit quality. The fruit ripens late winter into spring and is best picked when fully colored, as flavor improves with hang time.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, drained site.

directions: A richly flavored Willowleaf-type mandarin that can bear in alternating heavy and light years. Plant in full sun with good drainage and thin fruit in heavy years to encourage more even cropping. The deep-orange, seedy fruit ripens in winter; harvest when fully colored and the flavor is intense and sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: This old Mediterranean mandarin has slender, willow-like foliage and a graceful, weeping habit. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil and keep it evenly watered for good fruit fill. The small, richly aromatic, seedy fruit ripens in winter; harvest when fully colored, as flavor deepens on the tree.

planting.note: Transplant in spring in a warm location.

directions: A leading Spanish clementine prized for sweet, nearly seedless, easy-peeling fruit. Grow in full sun with excellent drainage and steady moisture, avoiding nearby cross-pollinators to keep it seedless. Fruit ripens in early winter; harvest when the rind is deep orange and slightly loose from the flesh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny spot.

directions: One of a trio of late University of California mandarins, Shasta Gold bears large, seedless, richly sweet fruit. Plant in full sun with good drainage and provide consistent water and feeding. The fruit ripens late winter into spring and holds well on the tree; harvest once fully colored and sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost passes.

directions: A large, seedless, deep-orange mandarin with a rich, sweet flavor and easy-peeling rind. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and keep evenly watered during the long ripening period. The fruit matures late winter into spring; harvest when fully colored and the segments taste sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring in warm soil.

directions: This seedless mandarin ripens somewhat earlier than its Shasta and Yosemite siblings, with large, sweet, easy-peeling fruit. Plant in full sun with good drainage and give it regular water and feeding. Fruit ripens in mid to late winter; harvest when the rind is fully colored and the flavor is sweet and rich.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: A tangerine-grapefruit cross with large, deep reddish-orange, tart-sweet, very juicy fruit. Grow in full sun with well-drained soil and keep steadily watered for good fruit size. Fruit ripens winter into early spring and is seedy; harvest when fully colored and the juice is rich and tangy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sunny spot.

directions: Also sold as Ruby, this seedless grapefruit has a reddish rind blush and pink flesh. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and provide steady deep watering. Fruit ripens through winter and holds long on the tree; harvest gradually as it sweetens and the flesh deepens in color.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger.

directions: One of the older pink-fleshed grapefruits, Foster bears seedy fruit with an attractive rosy blush. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and abundant summer heat for good flavor. Fruit ripens through winter; harvest gradually as the flesh sweetens over the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: A Japanese pomelo bearing very large fruit with thick rind and mild, sweet, pink-tinged flesh. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and give it room, as the tree grows large and vigorous. Fruit ripens winter into spring; harvest when fully sized and the flesh has sweetened, as the rind stays greenish-yellow.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: A classic Italian lemon group grown for high juice and oil content and near-continuous cropping. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and feed regularly, as lemons are heavy feeders. Fruit is produced through much of the year with a main winter crop; harvest when the rind turns full yellow and slightly soft.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: An ornamental Eureka sport with cream-and-green variegated leaves and green-striped young fruit that ripens to yellow with pink-tinged flesh. Plant in full sun with good drainage and protect the tender growth from frost. It fruits nearly year-round; harvest when the striping fades to yellow and the flesh is juicy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm site.

directions: An early-ripening Italian lemon with elongated, smooth, thin-rinded fruit and few seeds. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and give it regular water and feeding. It produces its main crop in early autumn ahead of other lemons; harvest when the rind turns pale yellow and the fruit is juicy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: A vigorous lemon-type citrus with reddish-tinged, mildly acidic fruit, often used as rootstock but also grown for its abundant crop. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage; it is tolerant of a range of soils and quite productive. Fruit ripens through winter; harvest when the rind colors orange-yellow and the fruit is juicy.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates heat, drought, and light frost.

directions: This thorny Australian native is exceptionally tough, tolerating drought, heat, and light frost. Plant in full sun with very sharp drainage; it can form a large shrub or small tree and often suckers. The tiny, tart green limes ripen in summer and are used whole; harvest when the fruit softens slightly and the juice is sharp.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: A rainforest native from eastern Australia bearing round, thick-skinned green limes with tangy pulp. Plant in full sun to light shade with rich, well-drained soil and steady moisture. The fruit ripens in late summer to autumn; harvest when the limes soften slightly and the juice is aromatic and tart.

planting.note: Very cold hardy; plant in spring or fall in most temperate zones.

directions: This deciduous, extremely cold-hardy citrus relative has curved thorns and contorted stems that make it a striking ornamental and common rootstock. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; it tolerates hard freezes well below other citrus. The small, fuzzy, seedy fruit ripens in autumn and is intensely sour and aromatic, used mainly for marmalade rather than fresh eating.

planting.note: One of the hardiest true citrus; plant in spring.

directions: Among the most cold-tolerant of true citrus, this Chinese species survives brief dips near zero degrees Fahrenheit. Plant in full sun with good drainage; it forms a thorny, winged-leaf shrub or small tree. The lemon-like, seedy fruit ripens in autumn and is very acidic, valued mainly for hardy-citrus breeding and aromatic zest.

planting.note: Fairly cold tolerant; plant in spring.

directions: A cross of satsuma mandarin and kumquat, this compact, cold-tolerant tree bears rounded orange fruit with a sweet, edible rind. Plant in full sun with good drainage; its small size suits containers. Fruit ripens in winter and holds long on the tree; eat the whole fruit once the rind is deep orange and the pulp has sweetened.

planting.note: Cold tolerant like kumquats; plant in spring.

directions: A mandarin-kumquat hybrid bearing small, bell-shaped, bright orange fruit with a sweet rind and tart pulp. Plant in full sun with good drainage; the compact, ornamental tree does well in containers. Fruit ripens in winter and hangs decoratively for weeks; eat whole once fully colored, as the sweet peel balances the tangy flesh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered location.

directions: A popular Japanese citrus, likely a mandarin-orange hybrid, with large, deep-orange, juicy fruit and a sweet-tart flavor. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and provide steady moisture and feeding. The fruit ripens late winter into early spring; harvest when the rind is fully colored and peels away from the aromatic flesh.

planting.note: Set out a grafted tree once nights stay reliably warm and frost is well past.

directions: Plant the grafted graft-union above the soil line in a sunny, well-drained spot sheltered from wind. Keep young trees evenly moist but ease off water as flowering nears to encourage a good set. This saffron-colored Gujarati favorite ripens in early summer when the shoulders soften and the aroma turns rich and sweet.

planting.note: Transplant in late spring after the ground has thoroughly warmed.

directions: Dig a wide hole in fast-draining soil and settle the tree at its original nursery depth. Water deeply through the growing season and mulch to steady soil moisture, then reduce irrigation before bloom. This nearly fiberless West Bengal dessert mango is ready when the green skin yields softly and gives off a strong perfume.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather so roots establish before any cool spell.

directions: Give this vigorous late-season tree plenty of room and full sun in deep, well-drained ground. Water steadily while the large fruit swells and taper off as ripening approaches. A very large mango grown across eastern India and Bangladesh, it is picked when the skin lightens and the flesh softens near the stem.

planting.note: Set out in late spring; this cultivar often fruits young and even off-season.

directions: This precocious Thai variety fruits quickly and suits container growing, so keep it in full sun with sharp drainage. Water regularly during fruit development and prune to hold size after harvest. Known as the Miracle Mango for its tendency to bloom twice a year, it ripens sweet and nearly fiberless when the skin turns golden.

planting.note: Transplant after frost danger has fully passed and soil is warm.

directions: Plant in a sunny, sheltered spot with excellent drainage and keep the graft union clear of soil. Maintain even moisture as the elongated fruit fills, then reduce water ahead of ripening. This prized South Indian dessert mango, also called Himayat, is ready when the greenish skin softens and its aroma deepens.

planting.note: Set out in warm late-spring weather for strong root establishment.

directions: Give this South Indian tree full sun and deep, well-drained soil, watering consistently through the season. Ease back on irrigation as bloom nears to help fruit set. The round, fiberless fruit is picked when the firm green shoulders begin to soften and yield to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Plant once the last frost is safely behind and the soil is warm.

directions: Settle the grafted tree at nursery depth in a sunny, freely draining site. Water deeply and regularly, mulching lightly to conserve moisture, then reduce water before flowering. One of the earliest North Indian mangoes, it stays green-skinned even when ripe, so harvest by softness and its strong, sweet fragrance.

planting.note: Transplant in late spring once frost risk is gone.

directions: Plant this classic Florida variety in full sun with fast-draining soil and keep the union above grade. Water young trees regularly and thin heavy crops to prevent limb breakage. The small, bright red fruit ripens early in the season and is ready when the skin colors fully and the flesh softens.

planting.note: Plant after the warm season begins for a strong start.

directions: Give this vigorous late-season Florida tree full sun and deep, well-drained ground. Water steadily as the crop develops and reduce irrigation before ripening. A dependable, fibrous mango long used for canning, it is harvested when the greenish-yellow fruit softens at the tip.

planting.note: Set out in warm late-spring conditions after frost.

directions: Plant this late-maturing Florida selection in full sun with excellent drainage. Keep the tree evenly watered while fruit sizes, then dry it down slightly before harvest. Prized for extending the season into late summer, its firm, fiberless fruit ripens yellow with a red blush and softens when ready.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once soil is warm and frost has passed.

directions: This compact-growing Florida variety takes full sun and sharply drained soil. Water regularly through the growing season and trim to keep the canopy open. The colorful red-and-yellow fruit has a rich, spicy flavor and is picked when the skin colors and the flesh gives slightly.

planting.note: Plant in late spring for best establishment in warm soil.

directions: Grow this popular Thai variety in full sun with fast-draining soil, watering evenly as fruit develops. Reduce irrigation near ripening to concentrate sweetness. The small, elongated fruit is intensely sweet and aromatic, ready when the skin turns deep yellow and softens.

planting.note: Transplant once the last frost is past and nights are warm.

directions: Plant this leading Indonesian variety in full sun and deep, well-drained soil. Water steadily through fruit development and mulch to hold moisture, easing off before harvest. Its name means fragrant and sweet, and the green-skinned fruit ripens with soft, nearly fiberless flesh and a strong perfume.

planting.note: Set out in warm weather after all frost risk has ended.

directions: Give this West Javanese variety full sun and freely draining soil, watering regularly as fruit forms. Reduce water near ripening to build sweetness. Named for its lipstick-red blush, the small aromatic fruit is harvested when the skin brightens and the flesh softens.

planting.note: Plant in late spring for good warm-soil establishment.

directions: Plant Myanmar's celebrated Diamond Solitaire mango in full sun with sharp drainage. Keep young trees evenly moist and taper water before flowering. The fine-textured, fiberless fruit is prized for its rich flavor and ripens golden-yellow, softening when ready to pick.

planting.note: Transplant after frost, in warm spring soil.

directions: This Israeli variety grows well in full sun and well-drained soil, tolerating a range of conditions. Water regularly while fruit sizes, then reduce before harvest. The medium fruit ripens with a red-orange blush and sweet, mildly spicy, fiberless flesh that yields when ripe.

planting.note: Plant once the warm season is underway and frost has passed.

directions: Give this handsome Gujarati variety full sun and deep, well-drained soil. Water evenly through fruit development and ease off as ripening nears. Noted for its attractive blushed skin and rich flavor, the fruit is harvested when the shoulders soften and the aroma sweetens.

planting.note: Set out in late spring after the soil warms.

directions: Allow this large, vigorous Indian tree ample space and full sun in well-drained ground. Water regularly as the big fruit fills and reduce irrigation before harvest. Known for producing very large mangoes, it is picked when the green-to-yellow skin softens and gives off a sweet scent.

planting.note: Transplant in warm spring weather once frost risk ends.

directions: Plant this early western Indian variety in full sun with fast drainage. Keep young trees evenly moist and taper water as flowering begins. A popular juicing mango with a tangy-sweet flavor, it ripens early and is ready when the blushed skin softens.

planting.note: Plant in warm late-spring soil for a strong start.

directions: This Indian hybrid of Neelum and Alphonso wants full sun and well-drained soil. Water consistently through fruit set and reduce before ripening. A regular bearer that avoids the biennial cycle, it produces sweet, fiberless fruit picked when the skin turns orange-yellow and softens.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in warm spring conditions.

directions: Grow this Indian hybrid in full sun with sharp drainage, watering steadily as fruit develops. Ease off water before ripening to enhance flavor. Notable for its very thin, nearly absent seed, the sweet fiberless fruit is harvested when the skin colors and softens.

planting.note: Plant in late spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant this South Indian variety in full sun and deep, well-drained soil. Water regularly through the season, tapering off before flowering. Valued for its bright crimson blush and sweet flavor, the fruit is picked when the skin brightens and the flesh yields to a gentle press.

planting.note: Set out after frost danger passes and soil is warm.

directions: Give this South Indian selection full sun and freely draining soil. Water evenly while fruit sizes and reduce irrigation before harvest. A firm, keeping mango grown in the Deccan region, it is picked when the green-yellow skin softens slightly and the aroma sweetens.

planting.note: Transplant in warm late-spring weather.

directions: Plant this South Indian variety in full sun with good drainage and water regularly. Reduce irrigation near ripening to concentrate flavor. The distinctive flat, rounded fruit has sweet, mild flesh and is harvested when the greenish skin softens and gives slightly.

planting.note: Plant in late spring after frost has cleared.

directions: This late-season Florida variety takes full sun and well-drained soil. Water steadily during fruit development and reduce as harvest nears. A dependable, disease-resistant tree, it produces yellow fruit with a light blush that softens when ready to pick.

planting.note: Set out in warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Give this vigorous Florida tree plenty of room and full sun in deep, well-drained soil. Water regularly as the long fruit fills and reduce before harvest. Known for its elongated, S-shaped fruit and long ripening window, it is picked when the yellow-and-red skin softens.

planting.note: Transplant once nights stay warm and frost is past.

directions: Plant this Southeast Asian variety in full sun with fast-draining soil. Water evenly through fruit development and ease off before ripening. The small, sweet fruit is often enjoyed both green and ripe, and is harvested when the skin turns yellow and softens.

planting.note: Plant in late spring for warm-soil establishment.

directions: This reliable polyembryonic variety grows well in full sun and well-drained soil. Water regularly as the crop develops, tapering off before harvest. A dependable bearer often grown from seed true to type, it produces sweet yellow fruit that softens when ripe.

planting.note: Set out in warm weather after the last frost.

directions: Give this Hawaiian variety full sun and deep, well-drained soil, watering steadily as fruit sizes. Reduce irrigation before ripening to build sweetness. The large, colorful fruit has firm, fiberless flesh and is picked when the red-and-yellow skin softens slightly.

planting.note: Transplant in late spring once soil warms.

directions: Plant this late-season Hawaiian variety in full sun with excellent drainage. Water evenly through fruit development and dry down before harvest. Valued for extending the season, its large firm fruit ripens with a red blush and fiberless flesh that yields when ready.

planting.note: Plant after frost, in warm spring conditions.

directions: This Hawaiian variety wants full sun and freely draining soil, with regular water while fruit fills. Ease off irrigation as ripening nears. The colorful, richly flavored fruit is harvested when the skin colors up and the flesh softens to gentle pressure.

planting.note: A compact tree well suited to containers; plant out after frost.

directions: This dwarf, condo-friendly Florida variety thrives in full sun and can be grown in a large pot with sharp drainage. Water regularly and prune lightly to maintain a compact form. The small fruit has rich, fiberless, coconut-toned flesh and ripens yellow with a red blush, softening when ready.

planting.note: Compact grower; set out once frost risk has passed.

directions: This small-growing Florida variety suits containers and small yards in full sun with well-drained soil. Water regularly through the season and prune to hold size. The tiny fruit packs a rich tropical flavor and is picked when the skin turns yellow and the flesh softens.

planting.note: A true dwarf ideal for pots; plant out after the last frost.

directions: This dwarf variety rarely exceeds a few feet and is well suited to container culture in full sun. Use a fast-draining mix, water regularly, and prune lightly to maintain shape. The small green-skinned fruit has smooth, creamy, fiberless flesh and is harvested when it softens and sweetens.

planting.note: Compact grower; transplant in warm spring weather.

directions: This compact Florida variety grows well in full sun and containers with sharp drainage. Water regularly as fruit develops and prune to keep the canopy manageable. Named for its distinctive sweet flavor, the small fiberless fruit ripens yellow and softens when ready to pick.

planting.note: Set out established suckers only after all danger of frost has passed and soil is warm.

directions: Plant a rhizome or sucker with its base a few inches below the soil surface in rich, well-drained ground, keeping the growing point just at grade. Feed heavily through the warm season and water deeply since these small sweet fingers form on a tall stalk. Harvest the fruit once the slender bananas plump and the ridges soften, letting them finish ripening off the plant.

planting.note: Its short, wind-firm trunk lets it go out early once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Set the pup in fertile soil so the corm sits an inch or two below grade, then mulch to hold moisture around the shallow roots. This stocky, cold-tolerant selection resists wind and produces chunky dual-purpose fruit good for eating fresh or cooking. Cut the bunch when the angular fruits round out and the first hand begins to yellow.

planting.note: Plant into warm soil after frost so this Cavendish-type establishes quickly.

directions: Plant a healthy division with the corm shallowly buried in deep, fertile soil and keep it sheltered from strong wind. A firm, Cavendish-group dessert banana, it rewards steady feeding and generous water with heavy bunches of sweet fruit. Harvest when the fruit is full and rounded but still green, and ripen the hands indoors.

planting.note: This vigorous Cavendish tolerates cooler subtropical settings but still needs a frost-free window to fruit.

directions: Bury the corm of a sucker just below the surface in rich, moisture-retentive soil and give it room, as this vigorous grower makes a tall pseudostem. Mulch heavily and feed with a high-potassium fertilizer to size the large bunches. Pick when the sweet dessert fruit is plump and angled edges have filled out, ripening it off the plant.

planting.note: A tall Cavendish-group banana that needs a long warm season to mature its bunch.

directions: Plant a sword sucker in deep, humus-rich soil with the corm just covered, staking tall plants against wind on their long stems. Keep it well fed and evenly watered so the slender, aromatic dessert fruit fills out fully. Harvest when the fruit rounds off and loses its sharp ridges, then ripen the hands away from the plant.

planting.note: A hardy, disease-tolerant cooking type set out once soil warms after frost.

directions: Set the corm shallowly in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch generously to conserve moisture and feed the mat. Bluggoe forms upright bunches of large, starchy, straight fruit prized for boiling and frying. Cut the bunch while the fruit is still green and angular for cooking, or let it turn for a firmer sweet flavor.

planting.note: This tall Brazilian Pome-type banana needs a long, warm growing season.

directions: Plant a vigorous sucker with the corm just below grade in deep, rich soil and give it plenty of room for its tall stalk. Widely grown in Brazil, it bears small to medium sweet-tart fruit with a firm texture that holds well. Harvest when the fruit is plump and the tips fill out, ripening the hands off the plant.

planting.note: A prized South Indian plantain-type banana planted into warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant a sucker or tissue-cultured plant with the corm shallowly covered in fertile, well-drained soil and feed heavily. This Kerala favorite yields long, tapered fruit that is eaten cooked when green and ripe alike, notably fried as chips. Harvest the bunch when the fruit is full and edges begin to round, using it green for cooking or letting it ripen for a sweeter table fruit.

planting.note: A hardy, widely grown South Indian dessert banana set out in warm soil.

directions: Set the corm just below the soil line in deep, fertile ground and keep the mat mulched and well fed. Poovan produces long, dense bunches of small, firm fruit with a slightly acidic, aromatic sweetness. Cut the bunch when the fingers plump and the first hand yellows, ripening the rest off the plant.

planting.note: A rugged, drought-tolerant South Indian banana planted once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant a sucker with the corm shallowly buried in well-drained soil, as this vigorous type tolerates poorer ground and less irrigation than most bananas. It carries big bunches of small, powdery-sweet fruit with a distinctive camphor-like aroma. Harvest when the fruit fills out and the peel begins to soften, letting it finish ripening for full sweetness.

planting.note: A robust South Indian cooking banana set out into warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in rich, moisture-retentive soil and feed the mat generously through the warm season. Monthan bears stout, angular, starchy fruit used for cooking, chips, and the tender flower and stem in the kitchen. Cut the bunch while the fruit is still green and firm for cooking uses.

planting.note: A disease-resistant Honduran hybrid planted into warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant a sucker with the corm just covered in fertile, well-drained soil and keep it fed and watered for fast, sturdy growth. This tough FHIA hybrid resists Sigatoka and Panama diseases and gives large bunches useful for both cooking green and eating ripe. Harvest when the fruit rounds out, using it green as a vegetable or ripening it for a mild sweet flavor.

planting.note: A vigorous disease-resistant plantain hybrid planted after all frost has passed.

directions: Set the corm shallowly in deep, fertile soil and mulch well, giving this tall grower room and wind protection. Bred in Honduras for disease resistance, it produces plantain-type fruit that cooks like a true plantain when green or ripe. Harvest the bunch green and firm for frying or boiling, or let it turn to soften the starch.

planting.note: A dessert-type FHIA hybrid set out into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant a healthy division with the corm just below grade in rich, moist, well-drained soil and feed heavily. This FHIA dessert hybrid resists leaf-spot diseases and produces sweet, Cavendish-like fruit on a manageable plant. Harvest when the fruit is full and rounded, ripening the hands indoors until yellow.

planting.note: A hardy dual-purpose cooking hybrid planted once soil warms after frost.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in fertile soil and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist. This vigorous, disease-tolerant FHIA hybrid serves as a cooking banana green and eats sweet when fully ripe. Cut the bunch green for a starchy vegetable, or allow it to ripen on the hand for dessert use.

planting.note: A small-fruited Philippine dessert banana set out after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant a sucker with the corm just below the surface in rich, well-drained soil and feed regularly. This popular Philippine cultivar bears tight bunches of very short, thin-skinned, intensely sweet finger bananas. Harvest when the little fruits plump and the peel thins, letting them finish ripening off the plant.

planting.note: A prized South Indian Silk-type dessert banana planted into warm soil.

directions: Set the corm shallowly in fertile, well-drained soil and stake the bunch, as this Silk-group type can bear heavy hands on a slender stem. It produces highly aromatic, sweet-acidic fruit that is a premium table banana in southern India. Harvest when the fruit is well filled and the first hand colors, ripening the rest off the plant.

planting.note: A common Philippine apple-flavored dessert banana set out after frost.

directions: Plant a sucker with the corm just covered in rich, moist soil and feed steadily through the growing season. This widely grown Silk-type banana bears rounded, thin-skinned fruit with a sweet, slightly tart apple-like flavor. Harvest when the fingers round out and the peel thins, then ripen the hands indoors until fully yellow.

planting.note: A hardy Latin American cooking banana planted once soil is warm and frost-free.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in fertile, well-drained ground and mulch to conserve moisture around the mat. Grown widely in the Andes, Cachaco is a robust Bluggoe-type banana with straight, starchy fruit favored for boiling and soups. Cut the bunch while the fruit is green and firm for cooking, as it holds its texture well.

planting.note: A small sweet Brazilian dessert banana set out into warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant a sucker with the corm just below grade in rich, well-drained soil and feed lightly but regularly. This diminutive Brazilian cultivar bears short, golden, very sweet fingers considered a delicacy. Harvest when the small fruits plump and the skin thins, ripening the hands off the plant until deep yellow.

planting.note: A popular Malaysian dessert banana planted into warm soil after frost.

directions: Set the corm shallowly in fertile, moist, well-drained soil and feed generously through the warm months. A leading Malaysian dessert type, it bears firm, sweetly fragrant fruit with an orange-tinged flesh. Harvest when the fruit is plump and the ridges round off, ripening the hands indoors until yellow.

planting.note: A tall green-skinned Malaysian banana needing a long warm season to fruit.

directions: Plant a vigorous sucker with the corm just covered in deep, rich soil and give it room for its tall stalk. Pisang Nangka keeps a greenish-yellow peel even when ripe and offers firm, tangy-sweet flesh good fresh or cooked. Harvest when the fruit fills out and softens slightly, judging ripeness by feel rather than color.

planting.note: A South Indian hill banana that fruits slowly and needs a long frost-free season.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in deep, fertile, well-drained soil at a warm elevation and feed the mat steadily. Traditionally grown in the Palni Hills, it bears aromatic, sweet-acidic fruit prized for its distinctive flavor. Harvest when the fruit is well filled and begins to soften, ripening the hands off the plant.

planting.note: A South Indian hill dessert banana set out into warm, frost-free soil.

directions: Plant a sucker with the corm just below grade in rich, well-drained soil and keep it evenly watered and fed. This Tamil Nadu hill cultivar bears short, sweet, thin-skinned fruit noted for its keeping quality and pleasant aroma. Harvest when the fingers round out and the first hand colors, ripening the rest indoors.

planting.note: A premium Sri Lankan Silk-type dessert banana planted into warm soil.

directions: Set the corm shallowly in fertile, moist, well-drained soil and support the bunch on its slender stem. A prized Sri Lankan dessert banana, Kolikuttu bears aromatic, honey-sweet fruit often reserved for special occasions. Harvest when the fruit is well filled and the peel begins to yellow, ripening the hands off the plant.

planting.note: Set out the grafted tree once the soil has warmed and all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Plant the graft union several inches above the soil line in a wind-sheltered spot with fast-draining ground. This Israeli green-skinned variety carries a type B flower, so pair it with a type A tree for reliable fruit set. Harvest the thin-skinned pears while still firm and let them soften off the tree indoors.

planting.note: Plant after the last spring frost so young growth hardens off before the following winter.

directions: This Texas selection is among the most freeze-resistant avocados, tolerating brief dips near 15 F once established. Set the tree slightly high in well-drained soil and mulch the root zone heavily for winter protection. The thin green fruit ripens in late summer and is best eaten soon after softening.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once frost has passed to give the tree a full season to root in.

directions: A hardy Texas variety reputed to withstand temperatures around 15 to 18 F on a mature tree. Give it deep, quick-draining soil and protect the trunk during hard freezes in the first few winters. The small, egg-shaped purple-black fruit has a rich, nutty flavor and ripens in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost so the tree establishes before winter cold arrives.

directions: Also sold as Del Rio, this is one of the hardiest avocados, surviving brief lows near 15 F on established wood. Choose a sunny, sheltered site with sharply drained soil and mulch well for the first winters. The small green fruit ripens in autumn with thin skin and buttery flesh.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost so the young tree gains size before the next cold season.

directions: A compact, cold-hardy Texas variety that stays smaller than most avocados, making it easier to protect. It tolerates brief cold near 15 F once mature; keep the root zone mulched through winter. The green fruit softens in late summer and is prized for its creamy, mild flesh.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost to allow a full growing season for establishment.

directions: A hardy Gulf Coast selection able to shrug off short freezes near 15 F on mature trees. Give it deep, fast-draining soil in full sun and shelter it from drying winter wind. The dark, thin-skinned fruit ripens in fall and is eaten once it yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Transplant after spring frost so new growth matures before winter.

directions: A vigorous cold-tolerant variety rated to roughly 15 F when fully established. Plant with the graft high in loose, well-drained soil and wrap the trunk during the first hard freezes. The purple-black fruit reaches eating quality in fall and softens off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost has passed for best establishment.

directions: A dependable cold-hardy variety that handles brief lows near 15 to 18 F on mature wood. Site it in full sun with excellent drainage and mulch the roots heavily for winter. The green fruit ripens late summer to fall with smooth, rich flesh.

planting.note: Set out the grafted tree after frost danger passes and soil warms.

directions: A California Guatemalan variety with a long summer harvest and a tendency toward alternate bearing. Plant in full sun with fast drainage and thin heavy crops to encourage more even yearly production. The pebbly green fruit is left on the tree until size is full, then ripened indoors.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in warm, well-drained ground.

directions: An old Guatemalan variety producing large, dark purple fruit with thick, warty skin. Give it a sheltered, frost-free site and steady moisture during fruit development. Pick when the fruit has reached full size and let it soften off the tree before eating.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost, choosing a warm sheltered spot.

directions: A late-season Florida Guatemalan variety with somewhat better cold tolerance than most greenhouse types. Plant in full sun with good drainage and keep the young tree evenly watered. The pear-shaped green fruit holds well on the tree into winter and softens after picking.

planting.note: Plant after frost in warm, fast-draining soil.

directions: A productive Florida Guatemalan variety named for its heavy crops of glossy green fruit. Give it full sun and protect it from wind, which can strip flowers and young fruit. Harvest in fall when the fruit reaches mature size, then ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant only in frost-free warmth, as West Indian types have little cold tolerance.

directions: A classic South Florida West Indian variety bearing large, round green fruit with a mild, low-oil flesh. It needs a truly frost-free, humid setting and steady moisture through the growing season. Pick when fruit reaches full size in late summer and ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, frost-protected location.

directions: A spreading, low-growing Florida variety valued for reliable production of smooth green fruit. Its wide, dense canopy makes it easier to net or manage than tall types. Grow in full sun with good drainage and harvest in late summer, ripening the fruit indoors.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, sheltered spot with fast drainage.

directions: A Florida hybrid producing long, green, elongated fruit over a fairly early season. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and keep young trees staked against wind. Harvest the fruit at full size in summer and allow it to soften off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring in frost-free warmth.

directions: A late-season Florida variety that extends the harvest window into winter. Give it a warm, wind-sheltered site and steady moisture as fruit develops. Leave fruit on the tree until size is full, then ripen off the branch before eating.

planting.note: Plant only where frost is absent, in warm, fast-draining soil.

directions: An early-season Florida West Indian variety bearing large, smooth green fruit with mild flesh. It needs a frost-free, humid climate and consistent watering. Harvest starts in mid to late summer; pick at full size and let the fruit soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, protected location.

directions: A Florida variety known for very late fruit that hangs on the tree well into spring. Grow it in full sun with sharp drainage and shelter from frost. Its extended holding season makes it useful for stretching harvests past most other avocados.

planting.note: Plant in spring in frost-free, well-drained ground.

directions: A Florida hybrid producing dark, glossy fruit over a mid to late season. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and keep it protected from cold snaps. Harvest when the fruit reaches full size and ripen it off the tree until it yields to light pressure.

planting.note: Plant only in frost-free warmth with fast drainage.

directions: A striking West Indian variety producing very long, gourd-shaped green fruit with a small seed and mild flesh. It requires a truly tropical, frost-free setting and even moisture. Harvest the elongated fruit at full size in late summer and soften off the tree.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: A Puerto Rican selection widely grown in Florida for its large, heavy green fruit. Give it a frost-free location in full sun with good drainage and consistent water. Harvest in fall when fruit reaches full size, ripening it off the tree before use.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, frost-free spot.

directions: A late-season Florida variety bearing large, pear-shaped green fruit into winter. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and protect from frost during cold snaps. Its long holding season on the tree helps extend the fresh harvest window.

planting.note: Plant in frost-free warmth once soil has warmed.

directions: A Hawaiian variety producing rich, oily green fruit with a small seed over a long fall-to-winter season. It suits humid, frost-free climates and appreciates steady moisture. Harvest at full size and ripen off the tree for the best buttery texture.

planting.note: Plant in a warm, frost-free site with good drainage.

directions: A Hawaiian variety valued for its large, glossy green fruit and reliable bearing. Grow it in full sun in a humid, frost-free climate with even watering. Pick the fruit at full size and let it soften indoors before eating.

planting.note: Plant in frost-free warmth with excellent drainage.

directions: A Hawaiian variety producing thick-skinned green fruit with rich, high-oil flesh over the winter months. It performs best in warm, humid, frost-free conditions with steady moisture. Harvest at full size and ripen off the tree until it gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Plant after frost so the tree establishes before winter.

directions: An early-ripening Mexican-race variety from California with modest cold tolerance for the group. Plant in full sun with fast drainage and shelter it from hard freezes while young. The small green fruit ripens in early summer and softens off the tree.

planting.note: Set the grafted tree out after the last hard freeze once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant the graft union above the soil line in deep, well-drained ground and give it a compatible pollinator such as a male-flowering variety so the flesh turns dark and sweet. Water steadily through the first two summers and keep a wide mulch ring clear of the trunk. The pollinated fruit browns internally and eats crisp and mild, like spice cake, when it colors fully.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost; its compact size also suits a large container.

directions: Choose a sunny spot with fast-draining soil and plant this naturally dwarf tree at the same depth it grew in the nursery pot. It is self-fruitful and non-astringent, so the flat orange fruit can be eaten firm straight off the branch. Thin heavy crops on young trees to keep the short limbs from bending or breaking.

planting.note: Plant the bare-root or potted tree in early spring as growth resumes.

directions: Work compost into the planting hole and site the tree where it gets full sun and shelter from hard freezes. Grow a pollinator nearby so seeds form and the round fruit develops dark, sugary, cinnamon-flavored flesh. Let the fruit hang until deep orange and slightly soft, then pick before the first heavy frost.

planting.note: Set out in spring; the small stature works well near patios and in tubs.

directions: Plant this compact Fuyu-type in full sun with the graft kept above grade in loose, well-drained soil. It bears large, flat, seedless fruit that is non-astringent and can be harvested while still firm and crunchy. Prune lightly in winter to build a strong, open frame that carries the heavy fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring in the warmest microclimate available.

directions: Give this productive Israeli variety full sun and deep, well-drained soil, planting after the danger of hard frost has passed. The small tomato-shaped fruit is astringent until fully ripe, so let it soften completely or treat it to remove tannins before eating. Commercially it is sold ripened and de-astringed as firm, sweet Sharon fruit.

planting.note: Plant in early spring once the ground is workable and frost has passed.

directions: Plant in full sun and rich, draining soil, staking young trees against wind while they establish. This non-astringent variety produces very large, deep-orange fruit that stays sweet and can be picked and eaten while still firm. Feed lightly in spring and thin the crop so branches are not overloaded.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in spring after the last freeze.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained site and include a pollinator so this variety sets seed and colors its flesh a spicy brown. The round, brown-speckled fruit is sweet and non-astringent when seeded and eats firm. Harvest when the skin turns fully orange and the interior has flecked with cinnamon-brown streaks.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep soil with room for a broad canopy.

directions: Site this vigorous tree in full sun with plenty of room, since it grows large and spreading. It carries very big, flattened orange fruit marked by a distinctive ring or constriction near the middle. The fruit is astringent, so let it ripen to a soft jelly texture before eating.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a reliable choice for the warm Gulf South.

directions: Plant this dependable, self-fruitful Texas favorite in full sun and well-drained soil after the last frost. It stays fairly compact and productive, bearing flattened, deep-red astringent fruit in abundance. Let the fruit soften fully on the tree or after picking so the tannins mellow into rich sweetness.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; tolerates cold far better than Asian types.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy American selection in full sun and give it room to reach full height. It is largely self-fruitful and sets sweet, orange fruit that ripens early to mid fall. Allow the fruit to become fully soft and jelly-like before eating, as it is intensely astringent until dead ripe.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the more cold-tolerant Asian persimmons.

directions: Plant this hardy Asian variety in full sun and well-drained soil where colder-zone gardeners want a kaki-type persimmon. It produces small to medium, squarish orange fruit with rich flavor and reliable crops. The fruit is astringent, so let it ripen soft before eating and harvest ahead of hard freezes.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early-ripening sport of Fuyu.

directions: Plant this early Fuyu sport in full sun and fast-draining soil after the last frost. It ripens ahead of standard Fuyu, giving sweet, flat, non-astringent fruit that can be eaten firm and crisp. Thin young crops and stake against wind while the tree builds a sturdy framework.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after frost in a hot, sunny spot.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping this shrub on the dry side once established since it fruits best with summer heat. The fruit is notably sweet with a pink blush and soft, mild arils that appeal even to those who find other pomegranates too tart. Harvest when the rind turns fully colored and the fruit sounds metallic when tapped.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early-ripening bud sport of Wonderful.

directions: Site this early variety in full sun with good drainage and water deeply but infrequently through the growing season. It ripens weeks ahead of Wonderful, producing crimson fruit with sweet, less-tart red arils. Pick promptly when colored, as fully ripe fruit left on the bush may split after rain.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a cold-hardy Central Asian selection.

directions: Plant this hardy variety in a warm, sunny, well-drained location where its extra cold tolerance helps at the cool edge of pomegranate range. It bears medium fruit with sweet-tart red arils and ripens reliably in shorter seasons. Mulch the base in the first winters and prune out frost-damaged wood in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Grow this variety in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, watering deeply only during fruit development. It is prized for very sweet pink arils with unusually soft seeds and attractive orange-red flowers. Harvest when the rind flushes pink to red and eat fresh or juice for a mild, sugary drink.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sunny site.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and keep watering moderate to concentrate flavor in the fruit. This variety produces deep, dark-red fruit with rich, tangy-sweet arils and a strong classic pomegranate taste. Cut fruit from the bush with pruners once fully colored rather than pulling, to avoid tearing the branches.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a flavorful Turkmenistan-origin selection.

directions: Plant in full sun and fast-draining soil, allowing the shrub to dry between deep soakings once established. It bears fruit with distinctive dark purple-red arils and a sweet, complex, berry-like flavor. Ripe fruit colors deeply and its arils darken; harvest before autumn rains cause splitting.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in a hot, sunny position.

directions: Plant this upright, late-ripening variety in full sun and any well-drained soil, as jujubes thrive on heat and tolerate drought. Sherwood forms a narrow, symmetrical tree and bears large, sweet fruit that ripens in fall. Let the fruit turn fully mahogany-brown for the richest, date-like sweetness before picking.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a heat-loving, drought-tough tree.

directions: Give this vigorous variety full sun and well-drained soil, and expect it to sucker from the roots if not managed. It produces elongated, curved fruit named for its tapered shape, ripening late in the season. Eat the fruit crisp and apple-like when yellow-brown, or leave it to wrinkle and sweeten into a chewy date texture.

planting.note: Plant in spring; valued for both fruit and twisted branch form.

directions: Plant this ornamental variety in full sun where its dramatically zigzag, contorted branches can be appreciated. It bears small to medium, very sweet fruit and makes a striking specimen even in winter dormancy. Water sparingly once established and let the fruit brown fully for the best flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; noted among the sweetest jujube selections.

directions: Plant this high-quality variety in full sun and well-drained soil for maximum sugar development. It is celebrated for exceptionally sweet, elongated fruit and is often grown for fresh eating and drying. Allow the fruit to hang until fully brown and slightly soft, when the sugar content peaks.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early-ripening, flattened-fruit type.

directions: Plant this early variety in full sun and well-drained soil, useful where the season is a little shorter. It bears distinctive flattened, round fruit that is crisp, sweet, and good for fresh eating out of hand. Harvest as the fruit turns from green-yellow to reddish-brown, eating it while still crunchy.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; shade the trunk of young trees.

directions: Plant this Georgia selection in deep, moist, well-drained soil and protect the young tree's first two seasons with partial shade before easing it into full sun. Grow a second, genetically different pawpaw nearby for cross-pollination since the flowers are not self-fertile. The large, sweet, yellow-fleshed fruit is ripe when it softens and gives to gentle pressure in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early-ripening Canadian selection.

directions: Plant this early, large-fruited variety in rich, moisture-retentive soil and shade the sapling until it is established. Because pawpaws need cross-pollination, plant at least two different varieties within pollinating distance. NC-1 ripens ahead of many pawpaws with big fruit and few seeds; harvest as it softens and its aroma turns fragrant.

planting.note: Plant in spring; among the earliest pawpaws to ripen.

directions: Set this early variety in deep, fertile, well-drained soil and give young trees light shade before transitioning to full sun for best fruiting. Pair it with another variety for cross-pollination, since single trees rarely set fruit. It ripens early in the season with yellow, custard-like flesh; pick when the fruit yields softly to the touch.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist woodland-type soil.

directions: Plant this variety in rich, moist soil and protect the sapling with dappled shade its first summers before moving it to fuller sun. It bears fruit with deep golden-yellow flesh and mild, sweet flavor, and needs a second variety nearby to cross-pollinate. Harvest when the fruit softens and its skin lightens, eating soon after since ripe pawpaws keep only briefly.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Peterson Pawpaws release.

directions: Plant this named Peterson selection in deep, moist, well-drained soil, shading young trees until they are well rooted. It produces firm, flavorful fruit with a good flesh-to-seed ratio and reliable quality. Grow with another variety for pollination and harvest when the fruit softens and its fragrance develops.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a large-fruited Peterson selection.

directions: Plant this large-fruited Peterson variety in fertile, moisture-holding soil and give the young tree light shade before full sun. It is known for very big fruit with rich, sweet flesh and a firm texture that handles well. Pair it with a different variety for cross-pollination and pick when the fruit gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a flavorful Peterson selection.

directions: Plant this Peterson variety in deep, moist soil and shade the trunk of the young tree until it establishes. It bears fruit with rich yellow flesh and a well-balanced, aromatic flavor, and it needs another variety for pollination. Harvest each fruit as it softens and its skin develops a faint yellow cast, then eat or freeze the pulp promptly.

planting.note: Plant in spring; blooms in fall and fruits in late winter to spring.

directions: Plant this naturally dwarf, disease-resistant variety in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping the tree compact enough for small yards. It flowers in autumn, so protect the blossoms and developing fruit from hard freezes to secure a crop. The pale, juicy fruit ripens late winter into spring; harvest when it fully colors and softens.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, sunny position.

directions: Plant this variety in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil, siting it where fall blossoms are sheltered from freezing wind. It produces large, orange, richly flavored fruit that ripens in spring. Thin fruit clusters to increase size and pick when the skin turns deep orange and yields slightly to a squeeze.

planting.note: Plant in spring; noted for early-season ripening in warm climates.

directions: Plant this early variety in full sun and good draining soil in the warmest microclimate available. In mild-winter areas it can ripen unusually early, sometimes near the winter holidays, giving sweet orange fruit. Guard the fall bloom and young fruit from frost, and harvest when the fruit fully colors and softens.

planting.note: Plant in spring; prized for exceptionally large fruit.

directions: Plant this large-fruited variety in full sun and rich, well-drained soil, sheltering it from cold snaps during fall bloom. It is grown for some of the biggest loquat fruit, sweet and pale-fleshed with few seeds. Thin clusters for maximum size and harvest in spring when the fruit turns fully yellow-orange and soft.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees after the ground has warmed in spring.

directions: Plant the graft union a couple inches above the soil line in deep, well-drained ground and stake young trees against wind. Payne leafs out early and bears heavy lateral-fruiting crops, so protect emerging shoots from late frost. Harvest in fall once the green hulls split and nuts begin dropping.

planting.note: Transplant dormant grafted stock in early spring.

directions: Give Serr a hot, sheltered site with deep soil and space it widely to accommodate a vigorous, spreading canopy. It benefits from a compatible pollinizer and light thinning to prevent overcrop and sunburn on exposed nuts. Gather nuts in autumn as hulls loosen and split.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring once soil is workable.

directions: This mid-season, lateral-bearing variety forms a moderately sized tree that comes into production relatively young. Plant in deep, well-drained loam and keep young trunks shaded or painted to prevent sunscald. Harvest the large, light-colored kernels in fall as the hulls split open.

planting.note: Set out dormant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Tulare is a productive mid-to-late season type valued for well-sealed, high-quality nuts. Plant in deep soil with good drainage, provide a pollinizer such as Chandler or Franquette nearby, and thin overcrowded limbs for light penetration. Pick up nuts promptly after hull split to keep kernels bright.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring after frost danger eases.

directions: A compact, precocious variety that bears reliable crops of medium nuts and pollinizes several other walnuts. Plant in fertile, deep soil and prune lightly to maintain a strong open framework. Harvest in autumn as the hulls crack and nuts fall.

planting.note: Transplant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Pedro stays small and precocious, making it a useful pollinizer and a fit for tighter home orchards. Plant in deep, well-drained soil and stake until the trunk is established. Collect the plump, thin-shelled nuts in fall once hulls split.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the soil can be worked.

directions: This cold-tolerant, self-fertile English walnut leafs out late, helping it dodge spring frosts in cooler regions. Plant in deep, fertile soil and it will begin cropping while still young. Harvest the well-flavored nuts in fall as hulls open and drop.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in early spring.

directions: A late-leafing French variety prized for frost avoidance and heavy lateral bearing of quality nuts. Plant in deep, well-drained soil with a suitable pollinizer such as Fernette. Gather the light-kerneled nuts in autumn as the hulls split.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring.

directions: Lara is an early, precocious French variety that comes into bearing quickly and yields large, attractive nuts. Plant in deep soil with reliable drainage and provide a compatible pollinizer for full crops. Harvest in fall once hulls loosen and split.

planting.note: Plant dormant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: This selected black walnut is grown for easy-cracking nuts with a high kernel percentage. Plant in deep, fertile soil well away from garden beds sensitive to juglone. Harvest in fall after the hulls darken and soften, then cure the nuts before storage.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Emma Kay is noted for excellent cracking quality and full, well-filled kernels. Give it deep soil and full sun, keeping juglone-sensitive plants at a distance. Collect nuts in autumn as hulls soften, hull promptly, and dry before shelling.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring.

directions: A popular home-orchard black walnut that bears at a comparatively young age. Plant in deep, well-drained soil and keep the trunk protected while young. Harvest in fall as the hulls yellow and drop, then cure the nuts in a dry, airy spot.

planting.note: Transplant grafted stock in early spring.

directions: Sharkey produces notably large nuts on a vigorous, hardy tree. Set it in deep, fertile soil with plenty of room to spread and away from juglone-sensitive plantings. Gather nuts after hull softening in fall, hull them, and dry before storing.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring while dormant.

directions: Wichita is a protogynous variety best suited to warm, dry climates with a long season. Plant in deep soil and pair it with a protandrous pollinizer such as Western for good nut set. Harvest in fall as shucks split and release the thin-shelled nuts.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Choctaw yields large, well-filled nuts and shows good foliage health where the season is long enough to mature its crop. Plant in deep, well-drained soil with a compatible pollinizer nearby. Pick up nuts in fall once the shucks open and dry them for storage.

planting.note: Plant dormant grafted trees in spring.

directions: Mohawk is prized for very large nuts and early bearing, though it benefits from thinning to reduce alternate cropping. Plant in deep soil with a protogynous or protandrous partner for cross-pollination. Harvest in autumn as the husks split open.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Sioux produces small but exceptionally high-quality, well-filled kernels and shows good disease resistance. Plant in deep, fertile soil and provide a suitable pollinizer for consistent set. Harvest in fall as shucks open and nuts release.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Shawnee is a productive, early-bearing variety with attractive, well-filled nuts. Plant in deep soil with good drainage and pair with a compatible pollinizer. Gather nuts in fall once the shucks split and dry them promptly.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring while dormant.

directions: A leading variety for hot, arid pecan regions, Western Schley bears heavily but tends toward alternate cropping. Plant in deep soil, irrigate through the dry season, and provide a protogynous pollinizer. Harvest in fall as the husks open.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Lakota combines strong scab resistance with high-quality kernels and good production. Plant in deep, well-drained soil and pair with a pollinizer of the opposite bloom type. Harvest in fall as the shucks split and release the nuts.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Mandan is an early-ripening variety with good scab resistance and attractive, well-filled nuts. Plant in deep soil with a compatible pollinizer for reliable set. Harvest in fall once the husks open, then dry the nuts before storage.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after hard frost.

directions: This French hybrid produces very large, easy-peeling nuts and shows strong resistance to gall wasp. Plant in slightly acidic, well-drained soil and provide a second chestnut for cross-pollination. Harvest in fall as the spiny burs open and drop their nuts.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Qing is a Chinese chestnut valued for sweet, high-quality nuts and blight resistance. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and pair with a compatible seedling or cultivar for pollination. Collect nuts in fall as the burs split, and refrigerate or cure them soon after harvest.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring.

directions: This vigorous hybrid of Chinese, American, and Japanese parentage grows quickly and bears good crops of medium-large nuts. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil with another chestnut nearby for pollination. Harvest in fall as the burs open and nuts fall free.
